To make sense of Russian politics, Brian Taylor grounds his discussion in an appreciation of some enduring influences, such as the primacy of the state over society and the role of the West, which has represented a source of inspiration, of threat, and of competition for generations of Russians. At the same time, most of the focus is on the period since the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991. That revolutionary event brought about dramatic change across multiple spheres, from the nature of the political and economic system to the country’s borders and who counts as a citizen. More than three decades later, Russia is still working its way through the consequences of these transformations.

titles.title: The Rule of Law
titles.author: Aziz Z. Huq
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: The Rule of Law: A Very Short Introduction sets out the origins and development of an English-language debate centered around the phrase “rule of law.” It aims is to explore the distinctive ethical contributions offered by various thinkers to this specific phrase, first theorized by the English scholar A. V. Dicey, while largely setting aside how the same questions are framed and resolved in other traditions. The book opens by canvassing the classical and early modern sources upon which Dicey and his successors explicitly drew. It then explores the ideas of Dicey, which it flags as the first self-conscious theorist of the rule of law. It then recounts his immediate successors. These include a heterogeneous range of thinkers, such as Friedrich Hayek, Lon Fuller, Ronald Dworkin, and Tom Bingham. With this genealogy in hand, the book then reflects the important question of why the rule of law (in some version or other) persists—that is, why do actors with the power to cast aside the rule of law not do so? The book next turns to the ways in which the phrase “rule of law “has diffused across borders, making it a geopolitical phenomenon. Specifically, the phrase was taken across borders toward the end of the twentieth century by actors as diverse as the World Bank, Singapore, and the Chinese Communist Party. Finally, the book closes by examining the way that the rule-of-law tradition can be challenged both as a matter of theory and practice.

titles.title: Social Science
titles.author: Alexander Betts
titles.subject: Social Science
titles.abstract: Social Science: A Very Short Introduction examines social science as the study of human behaviour. Social science provides tools to understand and explain people’s choices and actions while also considering how they live together in communities. The VSI explains how methods and theories from different disciplines can be applied and combined to address major global challenges. It further highlights how social scientists can have real-world impact and change the world for the better. It examines cutting-edge themes such as behavioural science, human data science, and the ways social science can work in collaboration with natural sciences and humanities.

titles.title: Sustainability
titles.author: Saleem H. Ali
titles.subject: Environment/Environmental Science
titles.abstract: Sustainability: A Very Short Introduction explores the concept of sustainability and its development. The growing concern about global environmental change and human impacts on the planet has led to the emergence of a broad field of study on the sustainability of human societies. The VSI then highlights the key concepts crucial to sustainability, such as the material and energy flows in consumption and production and the technological interventions for a sustainable society. Some of the important concepts include tipping points, resilience in natural and social systems, and renewability and circularity in the economy. The VSI also discusses the political means of managing anthropogenic change for a more sustainable society.

titles.title: Symbiosis
titles.author: Nancy A. Moran
titles.subject: Biological Sciences
titles.abstract: Symbiosis—the close association of unlike organisms—was long considered a curiosity, interesting but confined to a small proportion of species in nature and of little consequence. But discoveries over the past 25 years have made it clear that symbiosis has shaped evolution and has affected all species and ecosystems. Symbiosis enabled plants to colonize land, termites to eat wood, and corals to build reefs. At the other end of the evolutionary timescale, symbioses arise constantly and can play an active role in the ecology of populations, giving plants and animals the ability to overcome disease, heat stress, and nutritional deficiencies. Human cells are themselves amalgamations of organisms with different evolutionary roots, and gut symbionts play a central role in human health. Drawing examples from diverse species and ecosystems, Nancy Moran explains the main drivers that fuel the origins of symbiotic unions as well as the obstacles to their stable persistence. The roles of symbioses in shaping Earth and life are explored, along with their potential as tools in efforts to reduce negative effects of global change.

titles.title: Meaning
titles.author: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
titles.subject: Philosophy
titles.abstract: Our ability to see meaning in things, and to recognize the meaning those things have for others, is one of the most important aspects (indeed, perhaps the most important aspect) of human life. However, it is also one of the most mysterious. Where does meaning come from? Can we find a place for it in a naturalistic, scientific description of our world? What sorts of things can be the bearers of meaning? And how do we grasp the meanings others want to convey? This book explores these issues, focusing on the ways in which meaning arises in language, and showing how the decisions we make about meaning can have profound practical consequences.

titles.title: The Catholic Reformation
titles.author: James E. Kelly
titles.subject: Religion
titles.abstract: The Catholic Reformation unleashed bursts of creativity that changed the spiritual and, indeed, sensory experience of everywhere it touched. While on the one hand it offered a universality of experience, differences in interpretation and implementation as it adapted to cultural circumstance, particularly on its furthest geographical edges, created a two-way relationship between the Catholic world’s traditional centres and its new peripheries. The Catholic Reformation: A Very Short Introduction places this movement within its global context. It considers the expected, such as spiritual and religious matters, and how they entwined with the political, but also covers the full gamut of experience of the Catholic Reformation, particularly its deliberately sensory approach in terms of art, architecture, and music. The book traces the Catholic Reformation from its beginnings in the first half of the 16th century, pre-dating the Council of Trent (which is frequently viewed as its starting point), through the years of unrest in Europe, to its global expansion in the Americas, Asia, and Africa, and its continued influence and legacy into the 20th century. This book not only introduces the reader to the breadth of the Catholic Reformation experience in concise form, but it also sets it within important recent debates within the scholarship, thus presenting a provocative discussion of the topic’s central issues. Combining broad overviews and focused examples, The Catholic Reformation: A Very Short Introduction offers a concise introduction to a global movement.

titles.title: Sophocles
titles.author: Edith Hall
titles.subject: Classical Drama/Biography
titles.abstract: Sophocles: A Very Short Introduction presents the life, work, and influence of Sophocles the Athenian, who is known to be one of the greatest dramatists of all time. It provides an overview of the analysis concerned with the conventions of ancient Greek tragic theatre while also considering the historical context of Sophoclean drama plays. Sophoclean dramas survived to be read and widely performed into the 21st century. The VSI then highlights the distinctive features―tight plots, titanic personalities, and lucid style―of the plays. It also looks into the key concepts of sympathetic women, exquisite poetry, and stagecraft featured within Sophocles’ works.

titles.title: Toleration
titles.author: Andrew R. Murphy
titles.subject: Philosophy/Law
titles.abstract: Toleration is one of the foundational concepts of modern political life: both praised as an important first step toward securing rights and liberties and criticized as a half-measure that fails to secure equal respect and mutual concern. This Very Short Introduction canvasses the history, development, and contemporary status of toleration, laying out its conceptual foundations; exploring arguments advanced in its favor as well as those offered by its critics; and surveying the eras and regions where rulers and regimes have attempted to implement it as a way of dealing with various types of difference. Finally, it reflects on the prospects of toleration in a globalized, highly polarized, and ever more diverse world.

titles.title: Futurism
titles.author: Ara H. Merjian
titles.subject: Art
titles.abstract: Futurism: A Very Short Introduction presents the ideas, works, key figures, and development of the Futurist movement in Italy and beyond. As one of Western Europe’s least industrialized countries, Italy appeared impervious to developments of modern technology like motorcars and radio as it radically transformed urban life in the twentieth century. Moreover, Futurism holds significance in the pioneering development of new media. The VSI provides an overview of a nuanced account revolving around the relationship between Futurism and Mussolini’s Fascist regime. It also discusses the influence of Futurism on the art world, politics, and culture, and its subsequent continuing legacy.

titles.title: Mary Wollstonecraft
titles.author: E. J. Clery
titles.subject: Gender/Biography
keywords: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
titles.abstract: Mary Wollstonecraft: A Very Short Introduction discusses the life and legacy of Mary Wollstonecraft, who was widely hailed as the mother of modern feminism. It highlights the significance of her work A Vindication of the Rights of Woman. However, Wollstonecraft’s achievement as a thinker and writer reaches far beyond the text as she wrote about different topics and in a range of literary forms, some of which she created herself. The VSI then provides an overview of the conditions for Wollstonecraft’s emergence as a feminist and her subsequent reception. It also elaborates on Wollstonecraft’s status as an educator, a political thinker, and a romantic.

titles.title: Psycholinguistics
titles.author: Fernanda Ferreira
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Psycholinguistics: A Very Short Introduction discusses psycholinguistics as an accessible and engaging description of how people use language. Psycholinguistics refers to the study of how humans process language and how they store and retrieve linguistic information. On the surface, talking and understanding language seemed simple and straightforward. However, according to research in psycholinguistics, complex computations occur behind the scenes when people communicate with other people. This Very Short Introduction also provides an overview of recent debates revolving around how AI tools, such as ChatGPT, underscore some of the core questions about the language faculty and how humans comprehend, produce, and learn language.

titles.title: George Eliot
titles.author: Juliette Atkinson
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: George Eliot: A Very Short Introduction examines how Eliot pushed the boundaries of English fiction. An extraordinary woman whose unconventional life meant that she was often judged harshly, Eliot wanted to capture the feelings and motivations of ordinary people leading ordinary lives, and hoped that this might expand her readers’ powers of compassion and generosity. She believed her work might do good, but she was also clear-eyed about the limits of human sympathy and fiction. To convey the complexity of human behaviour, Eliot drew on an astonishing range of philosophical and scientific ideas and experimented with narrative voice, genre, imagery, structure, and syntax. This VSI considers how Eliot handled many of the recurrent preoccupations of her novels, such as the stifling nature of gossip, the constraints of provincial life, the challenges of practising fellow-feeling, the difficulty of transcending our limited perspectives, and how attending to individual and local needs might enable the gradual improvement of society. The book traces the ways in which a social outcast became a sage through the creation of probing and innovative novels that remain influential today.

titles.title: Agatha Christie
titles.author: Gill Plain
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Agatha Christie: A Very Short Introduction explores the enduring appeal of the world’s bestselling novelist, whose works have been translated into over 100 languages and adapted for stage and screen. Christie’s prolific career, spanning 1920 to the 1970s, includes plays, thrillers, middlebrow melodramas, domestic noir, and the clue-puzzle detective fiction that made her name. This VSI explores how Christie’s fiction works, and considers the curious alchemy of her style, characterization, and plotting, while also noting her playfulness and satirical edge. The book also examines Christie in context, looking at how her fiction refracted contemporary concerns and mapped changing tensions, anxieties, and desires across the century. It considers the success of her detectives, their ethical preoccupations and the themes of power, hypocrisy, deceit, and duality that underpin her narratives. It recognizes Christie as a psychologically complex writer, but also a woman of her time, and the book explores some of the challenges her writing presents. Yet, changing cultural attitudes have not diminished Christie’s phenomenal popularity, and the VSI examines the reinvention of Christie and the reimagination of her characters across screen and stage adaptations. There are good reasons for her global popularity and the book explores why Christie continues to captivate a diverse readership and inspire screenwriters worldwide.

titles.title: Henry David Thoreau
titles.author: Lawrence Buell
titles.subject: Literature/Philosophy/Biography
titles.abstract: Henry David Thoreau: A Very Short Introduction highlights Thoreau as a key figure in the American Transcendentalist movement and an influential thinker whose work continues to resonate globally. His masterpiece Walden, which chronicles his two-year experiment in simple living, blending nature writing with philosophical reflection, has inspired many similar experiments. His essay “Civil Disobedience,” recounting his arrest and incarceration for tax refusal, has inspired modern nonviolence resistance movements worldwide. Thoreau’s work provokes polarized reactions, however, from admiration for his environmental foresight to criticism of his alleged willful idiosyncrasy and self-focus. This VSI explores key themes in Thoreau’s writings, including self-reliance, nonconformity, political radicalism, environmental consciousness, scientific curiosity, and spiritual seeking, while acknowledging the controversies surrounding his life and legacy. It situates Thoreau within both his historical moment and broader literary, cultural, and intellectual traditions.

titles.title: Administrative Law
titles.author: Stephen Thomson
titles.subject: Law
titles.abstract: Administrative Law: A Very Short Introduction explores how administrative law shapes decision making across the public sector, from immigration and social welfare to taxation and public health. It highlights the mechanisms available to challenge decisions, including ombudsman complaints, tribunal appeals, and judicial review. This VSI examines core elements such as internal review, freedom of information, public inquiries, and anti-corruption measures. Drawing on examples from major common law jurisdictions—including the UK, Australia, Canada, the USA, India, and Hong Kong—it reveals both shared foundations and jurisdictional variations. Thomson offers a concise, comparative overview that illuminates the essential role of administrative law in modern governance.

titles.title: Alexander Hamilton
titles.author: R B Bernstein
titles.subject: History/Politics/Biography
titles.abstract: Alexander Hamilton: A Very Short Introduction examines the life of a revolutionary leader, constitutional thinker, and the nation’s first secretary of the treasury. He led the effort to author The Federalist, offering a brilliant defense of the Constitution, and championed its broad interpretation. This VSI presents his drive to build a strong national government capable of preserving the Revolution’s achievements and securing the nation’s survival. It captures his role as a soldier, legal expert, and political strategist who shaped American governance. Through relentless ambition and action, Hamilton embodied the energy essential for the country’s success.

titles.title: Black Narratives of Slavery
titles.author: Robert J Patterson
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Black Narratives of Slavery: A Very Short Introduction shows how slavery and its afterlife have shaped Black history, Black culture, Black politics, and Black freedom aspirations. It connects the pre-Emancipation era of slavery to forms of disenfranchisement, including Jim Crow segregation, to argue that American chattel slavery legally ends in 1865, but its legacies persist. This VSI brings together a wide range of sources, including oral, visual, and written, from the pre-Emancipation era to the twenty-first century to reveal how Black people and Black culture have used an array of strategies to respond to and fight against slavery and then other forms of discrimination. Finally, it insists that they do so not only to emphasize slavery’s enduring impact, significance, and meaning into the twenty-first century but also to imagine a world in which Black people possess their full citizenship rights and the power of anti-Black racism and white supremacy becomes less forceful.

titles.title: Cicero
titles.author: Yelena Baraz
titles.subject: Classical Studies/Biography
titles.abstract: Marcus Tullius Cicero shaped late republican Rome as an orator, statesman, and philosopher, defining public speaking and adapting Greek thought for Roman audiences. He rose to the highest political office, engaged in power struggles, and faced exile before his assassination. This Very Short Introduction explores Cicero's role as a mediator between theory and practice, philosophy and politics, Greek ideas and Roman tradition. His speeches influenced oratory for centuries, while his philosophical works established key foundations for later thinkers. By examining his intellectual and political life, this book reveals how overarching ideas connected his diverse contributions.

titles.title: Entropy
titles.author: James Binney
titles.subject: Physics
titles.abstract: Entropy is as important a concept as energy, but less well known. The two concepts were first distinguished in the middle of the 19th century. By 1900 entropy was understood to be a measure of disorder and been widely applied in physics and chemistry, but in 1947 it was discovered to be the correct measure of ignorance. In an increasingly data-driven world the ‘principle of maximum entropy’ is playing a key role in data analysis and decision making. Quantum mechanics is a scheme for computing probability distributions, which are quantified by entropy. ‘entanglement entropy’, which plays a key role in cutting-edge quantum technologies, is revealing aspects of entropy that are not evident in the classical world. The thermodynamics of gravitating systems such as star clusters profoundly differs from that of the every-day systems from which entropy emerged. From the thermodynamics of black holes, which involves relativity and quantum mechanics in addition to gravity, an idea, the ‘holographic principle’, emerges that calls into question our current model of material reality. This book traces the emergence of entropy and energy as key concepts and illustrates their practical importance by discussing aspects of the energy transition such as heat pumps, solar cells, carbon capture, and liquified natural gas. Then it explains how entropy quantifies ignorance and illustrates the resulting principle of maximum entropy by reference to image reconstruction. Chapters on the role of entropy in quantum mechanics and gravity follow.

titles.title: Environmental Justice
titles.author: Pamela Hill
titles.subject: Environmental Science/Politics
titles.abstract: This book examines what environmental justice is, the emergence of the environmental justice movement, governmental and international responses to environmental injustices, and the critical problem of climate justice. Environmental justice concerns the disproportionate environmental burdens suffered by underserved and marginalized people and the unfair distribution of environmental benefits to those with the influence and financial resources to enjoy them. It recognizes the responsibilities of empowered groups, large corporations, and industrialized countries to correct environmental harms, such as pollution and rising seas from climate change, that they have, unwittingly or not, inflicted on those who are less powerful. In the last several years, this important idea has gained center stage globally: the UN, governments, nongovernmental organizations, and academia now regularly address it. Drawing on the latest scholarship and highlighting examples from around the world, the book identifies the many challenges to environmental justice and reflects on how we might achieve it. It explores root causes of environmental injustice such as racism, capitalism, and colonialism, and suggests that environmental justice requires fundamental changes to our economic and governmental structures and to societal values that promote consumerism and profitmaking rather than the common good.

titles.title: Herman Melville
titles.author: Maurice S Lee
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: Herman Melville: A Very Short Introduction explores the life and work of one of America’s greatest writers. Best known for Moby-Dick, Melville crafted novels, experimental fiction, and poetry that confront economic inequality, slavery, science, and democracy’s fragility. This VSI traces his dramatic journey from privilege to poverty, his adventures at sea, his meteoric rise to fame, and his fall into obscurity after challenging social and artistic conventions. It examines how he reinvents himself as a poet and how his genius gains recognition only after his death. Melville’s restless ambition and bold imagination shape a literary legacy that remains vital today.

titles.title: Human Geography
titles.author: Patricia Daley, Ian Klinke
titles.subject: Geography
titles.abstract: Human Geography: A Very Short Introduction explores the dynamic ways in which space shapes global struggles over inequality, migration, racial and gender justice, and the climate crisis. It does so by examining spaces central to the functioning of the contemporary world, from the colony to the pipeline, and from the workplace to the conservation area. The authors even take their readers to outer space. In doing so, their book critically engages with the discipline’s history while emphasizing its ongoing relevance and transformative potential. By linking pressing global issues to spatial processes, this VSI provides a framework to understand and challenge existing power structures. The authors position human geography as vital to addressing the complexities of the modern world.

titles.title: Humanitarianism
titles.author: Julia F Irwin
titles.subject: Politics/History
titles.abstract: This book traces the events and trends that defined modern humanitarianism, from the eighteenth century up to the present day. It describes the origins of international humanitarian action, the development of major humanitarian organizations and movements, the evolution of international humanitarian law, and the expansion of the international humanitarian sector. It examines organized efforts to save lives, alleviate suffering, and maintain human dignity in the face of major conflicts, disasters, refugee crises, and other humanitarian emergencies. At the same time, it shows how beliefs about race, gender, religion, and nationality have historically influenced humanitarian sentiments and actions. Attuned to the distinctions between wartime, postwar, and nonconflict situations, it assesses criticisms waged against humanitarian actions and debates over the legitimacy of humanitarian interventions. It also teases out the complex relationship between humanitarianism, human rights, and international development. Finally, this book surveys diverse and competing humanitarian traditions throughout the world, placing humanitarian ideals and practices in a global context.

titles.title: The Iraq Wars
titles.author: Samuel Helfont
titles.subject: History/Politics
titles.abstract: American wars in Iraq dominated post-Cold War international relations for almost thirty years and will continue to shape global politics for the foreseeable future. The Gulf War of 1991, the American invasion of Iraq in 2003, and the campaign against the Islamic State beginning in 2014 each had its own logic. In many ways, they were distinct conflicts. However, something is lost by looking at them in isolation. Each war was fought to finish the previous conflict. The Iraq Wars, therefore, are perhaps best understood as a chain of events, one leading to the next. They swerved in many directions but are ultimately tied together by their closely intertwined history. This book relies of American and Iraqi sources, in both English and Arabic, to tie these wars together in a short, easily digestible volume. In doing so, it draws out the underlying causes of each war as well as the continuity between them.

titles.title: Jorge Luis Borges
titles.author: Ilan Stavans
titles.subject: Literature/Biography
titles.abstract: A probing, engaging examination of the work of Jorge Luis Borges (1899–1986) in the context of Argentinian, Latin American, and world literature, from his beginnings as an avant-gardist to his consecration as one of the most influential literary figures of the twentieth century. In essays, stories, and poems, Borges developed a unique voice that allowed him to explore favorite intellectual motifs: labyrinths, the infinite, the universe as a book, memory, and storytelling as fate. Just as he renewed the Spanish language by means of a tight, almost mathematical style, he redefined Argentine literature by inviting its practitioners to tackle universal themes, and he embraced translation as a strategy for reconceptualizing the original text. Ilan Stavans, a world authority on Borges and an internationally celebrated essayist and translator, reflects on what the term Borgesian means in an age of instant information, consumerism, and apathy toward reading.

titles.title: Mathematical Biology
titles.author: Philip K Maini
titles.subject: Biology/Mathematics
titles.abstract: Mathematical Biology: A Very Short Introduction explores how mathematical models uncover patterns in nature, such as animal coat markings (and their remarkable similarity to football shirt designs). It shows how biological ideas can be translated into the language of mathematics, where they can be integrated and studied in detail, generating results which can then be interpreted biologically. It introduces the key basic mathematical concepts used to analyse the models that arise from this procedure. It explains how the same mathematical principles apply across biological systems, from invasive species to cancer cells. This VSI illustrates how abstract mathematical thinking transfers knowledge between disciplines, improving scientific understanding. It demonstrates why mathematics is the language of science, challenging traditional approaches that rely solely on verbal reasoning. It shows that mathematical models can be used to test existing biological hypotheses and generate new hypotheses. Through numerous examples, it shows how modelling refines research in biology, medicine, ecology, and epidemiology, revealing insights that conventional methods often overlook. It also highlights what are the key future challenges in the field.

titles.title: Moses Maimonides
titles.author: Ross Brann
titles.subject: Philosophy/Religion/Biography
titles.abstract: Moses Maimonides: A Very Short Introduction highlights a philosopher, physician, scientist, rabbinic scholar, and community leader who transforms the pre-modern age. He reshapes Jewish thought and law with clarity and precision in The Guide for the Perplexed, his Commentary on the Mishnah, and the seminal Mishneh Torah. This VSI examines how Maimonides integrates a rationalist's methodology, inspired by his medical and scientific pursuits, into his philosophical and legal works. It explores his profound influence on Jewish and Western intellectual traditions, bridging reason and faith. Scholars and leaders draw enduring inspiration from Maimonides’ intellectual rigor and moral vision, securing his legacy as a timeless thinker.

titles.title: Neurodiversity
titles.author: Robert Chapman, Sue Fletcher-Watson
titles.subject: Psychology
titles.abstract: Neurodiversity: A Very Short Introduction explores the diverse ways individuals perceive, process, and respond to the world around them, shaping how they experience life and build relationships. It traces the development of the neurodiversity movement and situates it within a wider politics of collective liberation. This VSI examines neurodiversity across education, the workplace, and health and social care, highlighting both challenges and opportunities. The authors draw on current research and practice to advocate for more inclusive and equitable systems. Clear, concise, and deeply informed, it invites readers to rethink assumptions and recognize neurodiversity as a vital dimension of human difference.

titles.title: Oral History
titles.author: Douglas A Boyd
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: Oral History: A Very Short Introduction explores the dynamic field of oral history, from interviewing techniques to archival preservation. Douglas A. Boyd examines recording strategies, accessibility technologies, and the ethical and legal challenges inherent in oral history. This VSI highlights the interview as a balance of strategy and adaptability, memory and omission, narrative and silence. Boyd also examines archives and the expanding digital landscape, which enhance oral history’s accessibility for scholarly, public, community, and personal use. Offering a concise yet comprehensive overview, this book introduces readers to a rapidly evolving discipline that extends far beyond storytelling.

titles.title: Postwar Europe
titles.author: Richard Bessel
titles.subject: History
titles.abstract: The Second World War was the most destructive conflict in history. It left Europe in ruins, its economies severely damaged and millions of its people facing poverty and malnutrition, and its political systems shaken if not destroyed. Yet three decades later its economies had recovered; it largely appeared politically stable; and its inhabitants generally were well housed and well fed. Then at the end of the 1980s, the postwar settlement disintegrated, and a new chapter in Europe’s postwar history had begun. Postwar Europe: A Very Short Introduction offers a concise account of Europe’s history from the end of the Second World War to roughly the end of the 20th century. It explores conditions in Europe at the end of the war, how political life took shape and societies were rebuilt, the impressive postwar economic recovery, the Cold War division, the end of empire, and aspects of European cultural life during the second half of the 20th century. It explores how we might understand the beginnings and endings of the postwar era, and emphasizes the importance of the experiences of different generations of Europeans during the postwar decades.

titles.title: Thomas More
titles.author: Peter Marshall
titles.subject: History/Biography
titles.abstract: Thomas More is one of the most enduringly fascinating, as well as profoundly controversial, figures of English history. A brilliant scholar and writer, his Utopia of 1516 dared to imagine how society might be completely reordered. At the same time, his hatred of the Reformation caused him to advocate, and seek to implement, the death penalty for heretics. A friend and advisor to Henry VIII, More’s refusal to support Henry’s break with Rome brought about his own execution in 1535. The meanings of the life and death of Thomas More have always been contested. Courageous martyr or scheming traitor? Wise statesman-philosopher or facetious dilettante? Warm-hearted family man or sexually repressed misogynist? Champion of individual conscience or violent authoritarian? More is unique in having been hailed in the Soviet Union as a pioneer of communism, and canonized as a saint in the Roman Catholic Church. He is the hero of one of the 20th century’s most highly regarded historical films, and the villain of one of the 21st century’s most acclaimed historical novels. Peter Marshall offers an informed, insightful, and entertaining account of More’s life, writings, and achievements, along with an astute assessment of his posthumous reputation. Marshall argues that apparent contradictions in More’s character and actions must be embraced rather than explained away, and that modern people should accept the challenge of finding inspiration in historical figures whose values and attitudes differ radically from our own.
